column5: Content

Understand reliability engineering as the application of scientific, engineering, statistical, and mathematical methods to reduce the likelihood or frequency and consequences of unwanted product or system behaviours. Distinguish reliability from the complete elimination of failure and recognise that reliability is evaluated under specified operating conditions and over a defined period of time.
Identify and interpret the cumulative probability of failure by time, commonly denoted F(t), where T is the time to failure.
Identify and interpret the reliability function R(t) = P(T > t).
Visualise the failure and reliability functions and explain how probability of failure increases, and reliability decreases, over time.
Understand rated life as the operating time up to which a specified minimum reliability, or maximum acceptable probability of failure, must be maintained.
Explain how rated life can inform product replacement, inspection, maintenance, and servicing decisions.

Formulate a reliability requirement in terms of the maximum acceptable probability of failure, or minimum acceptable reliability, at a specified operating time and under a specified load or operating condition.
Understand an escape as a defective or insufficiently reliable product that passes through the organisation’s quality controls and reaches a later production stage or the customer.
Recognise failures occurring before rated life as potential reliability escapes and identify why such failures require investigation.
Associate early-life failures with causes such as manufacturing defects, assembly errors, or material flaws.
Associate normal-life failures with random events such as unexpected shock loads.
Associate end-of-life failures with degradation mechanisms such as wear, corrosion, and fatigue.
Understand quality engineering as the systematic design, control, assurance, and improvement of products and processes so that specified requirements are consistently met.
Distinguish quality engineering from inspection alone and recognise that quality must be designed and controlled throughout the product and manufacturing life cycle.
Understand performance as a dimension of total quality.
Understand provided features as a dimension of total quality.
Understand reliability as a dimension of total quality.
Understand durability as a dimension of total quality.
Understand conformance as a dimension of total quality.
Understand serviceability as a dimension of total quality.
Understand aesthetics as a dimension of total quality.
Understand perceived quality as a dimension of total quality.

Understand a turnback as a product, component, or process outcome that fails to satisfy an applicable quality criterion and must therefore be corrected, reworked, scrapped, or returned to an earlier stage.

Understand that selected product characteristics or key performance indicators are measured and compared with lower and upper specification limits. Recognise the interval from LSL to USL as the acceptable specification range and determine whether an individual unit conforms.
Calculate and interpret the scrap rate as the proportion of manufactured units outside the specification limits.
Calculate and interpret yield as the proportion of manufactured units within the specification limits.
Use parts-per-million (PPM) nomenclature to express low defect or non-conformance rates in high-volume production.
Represent variation in manufacturing outcomes using a probability distribution fitted to measurements of a product characteristic.
Relate the position and spread of a fitted distribution to specification limits and identify regions corresponding to conforming and non-conforming products.
Understand short-term yield as the proportion of the fitted process distribution expected to fall within specification limits under current process conditions.
Compare screening products through inspection with improving the underlying manufacturing process as approaches to preventing defective products from reaching customers.
Explain why 100% inspection does not necessarily guarantee zero escapes, including measurement errors, false acceptances, false rejections, and missed defects.
Define the process potential index Cp as a continuous measure of ability to conform to a specification, representing allowable specification spread relative to process distribution spread.

Define the process capability index Cpk as a continuous measure of ability to conform to a specification while accounting for process mean shift; interpret it using the lower and upper capability components.

Interpret indicative capability values: below 1.00 means process spread exceeds the specification range; about 1.33 is limited or conventionally acceptable; about 1.67 is good; and about 2.00 is very high capability.
Recognise that the required capability threshold depends on industry, product criticality, customer requirements, and consequences of failure.
Read and interpret process capability report cards, including specification limits, process centring and spread, capability indices, and expected or observed defect rates.
Understand that meeting specification limits does not necessarily mean a product has high performance or provides high customer value.

Explain that quality includes selecting appropriate performance requirements and consistently achieving them, whereas capability indices primarily evaluate consistency relative to existing specifications.
Recognise that capability indices based on short-term estimates of process mean and standard deviation may not represent long-term production performance.
Recognise that process drift, tool wear, environmental changes, material variation, and systematic trends must be detected and controlled to maintain long-term quality.

Give examples of visual inspection and evaluate its speed, cost, repeatability, measurement detail, and suitability for process control. Recognise that it may be fast and sufficient for obvious features but is not repeatable or quantitative.
Give examples of metrology or direct measurement and evaluate their advantages and limitations for quantitative assessment and process control.

Give examples of go/no-go gauges and evaluate their advantages and limitations. Recognise that they efficiently classify dimensional KPIs as conforming or non-conforming but do not quantify the measured value.
Understand production control as a feedback process in which measured outputs are compared with desired values and corrective actions are applied.
Recognise that correction will necessarily produce scrap if the only inspection methods are visual and go/no-go.
Explain how direct measurement of a KPI can reveal trends and support earlier intervention.
Understand control charts as process-control tools on which measurements and control limits can be plotted.
Understand the importance of collecting process inputs and outputs to investigate variation and identify potential causes of process shifts.
Interpret a correlation matrix and recognise that correlation can support investigation without by itself establishing causation.
Define production rate as the number of acceptable units produced per unit time.
Define takt time as the maximum available production time per unit required to satisfy customer demand.
Explain that net production rate must meet or exceed demand rate, or equivalently that cycle time must remain below takt time.
Identify use of an incorrect part as a source of manufacturing or assembly defects.
Identify omission of a part as a source of manufacturing or assembly defects.
Identify incorrect installation as a source of manufacturing or assembly defects.
Identify equipment malfunction as a source of manufacturing or assembly defects.
Identify material variation as a source of manufacturing or assembly defects.
Identify contamination as a source of manufacturing or assembly defects.
Identify handling damage as a source of manufacturing or assembly defects.
Recognise that increasing production speed may increase defect rates and reduce net production.
Understand that achieving high quality requires consistency, which rigorously applied standard operating procedures can help achieve.
Describe an inspection-centred approach as detecting defective units after production.
Describe a process-centred approach as preventing defects by controlling the factors and process inputs that generate them.
Understand how process maps support a process-centred approach to quality.
Identify activities in a process map.
Identify decisions in a process map.
Identify inputs in a process map.
Identify outputs in a process map.
Identify rework loops in a process map.
Understand how Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) supports a process-centred approach to quality.
Identify failure modes in an FMEA.
Identify effects in an FMEA.
Identify causes in an FMEA.
Identify and interpret severity in an FMEA.
Identify and interpret occurrence in an FMEA.
Identify and interpret detection in an FMEA.
Identify existing controls in an FMEA.
Identify recommended actions in an FMEA.
Understand Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) as a statistical method used to determine whether one or more categorical predictors have a statistically significant effect on a continuous response variable.
Explain the meaning of the acronym ANOVA.
Identify situations in which ANOVA is appropriate, including comparison of mean performance across groups, datasets, treatments, machines, materials, or operating conditions.
Recognise that ANOVA evaluates differences between group means by comparing predictor-associated variation against unexplained or random variation.
Distinguish one-way, two-way, and higher-order ANOVA according to the number of predictors included.
Recognise that ANOVA can investigate individual predictor effects and, when applicable, interactions between predictors.
Understand one-way ANOVA as a model with one categorical predictor and one continuous response, where the observed response combines an overall mean, a treatment or group effect, and random error.
Identify the response variable, categorical predictor, and groups or treatment levels.
Formulate the null hypothesis that all population group means are equal.
Formulate the alternative hypothesis that at least one population group mean differs from the others.
Express the significance and risk levels and the sample sizes used for the ANOVA.
Understand that ANOVA separates total response variation into variation explained by differences between groups and residual variation remaining within groups.
Interpret source of variation, sum of squares, degrees of freedom, mean square, F-statistic, and p-value.
Understand sum of squares as a measure of variability around a reference mean.
Distinguish predictor-associated between-group variation from residual or unexplained within-group variation.
Understand that the F-statistic compares explained variation with residual variation.
Compare the p-value with a selected significance level, commonly 0.05.
Reject the null hypothesis when the p-value is below the significance level.
State that there is insufficient evidence to reject the null hypothesis when p is greater than or equal to the significance level; do not describe this as proving or accepting the null hypothesis.
Recognise that a significant ANOVA indicates at least one mean differs but does not identify which groups differ without further analysis such as post-hoc comparisons.
Assess ANOVA model adequacy before drawing conclusions from sums of squares, F-statistics, or p-values. Statistical conclusions are reliable only when the model reasonably represents the data.
Interpret residuals as differences between observed and model-predicted values.
Examine residual plots to assess whether errors are randomly distributed around zero.
Check whether residual variability is approximately constant across fitted values and groups.
Assess whether residuals are approximately normally distributed, while recognising that apparent non-normality may result from small sample size.
Check residual independence, particularly for observations collected over time.
Identify outliers and recognise that they require engineering and statistical investigation rather than automatic removal.
Recognise curved or systematic residual patterns as possible evidence of missing terms, incorrect model structure, needed transformations, or unrepresented relationships.
Recognise residual trends over time as possible evidence of drift, degradation, environmental effects, or omitted time-related predictors.
Consider adding relevant factors or interactions when important sources of variation appear omitted.
Consider transformations such as natural logarithm or Box-Cox when variance or distributional assumptions are not satisfied.
Use mean square error and adjusted coefficient of determination as supplementary indicators of model adequacy.
Understand two-way ANOVA as an extension of one-way ANOVA involving two categorical predictors and one continuous response variable.
Appreciate the possibility of including an interaction term in a two-way ANOVA.
Understand that observed variability in manufacturing or experimental data contains both underlying process variability and measurement-system variability.
Use the relationship: Observed Variability = Process Variability + Measurement Variability.
Recognise that when measurement variability is similar in magnitude to process variability, incorrect decisions may result and the measurement system must be analysed.
Explain that measurement-system analysis should identify the magnitude and contributors of measurement error, system stability, and pathways for improvement.
Understand the principal characteristics used to evaluate a measurement system and interpret a Gauge Repeatability and Reproducibility study.
Define accuracy as closeness of a measurement or average measurement to an accepted reference value.
Define bias as the systematic difference between the average measured value and the accepted reference value.
Define resolution as the smallest increment or change that the measurement system can display or distinguish.
Define repeatability as variation when the same operator measures the same part repeatedly with the same instrument, method, and conditions.
Define reproducibility as variation associated with changes in operators, instruments, laboratories, fixtures, or other measurement conditions.
Define stability as changes in measurement bias or variation over time.
Define linearity as changes in measurement bias across the operating range of the instrument.
Understand that a repeatable system has little standard deviation among successive measurements of the same part by the same person with the same instrument.
Understand that reproducibility means little variation across different conditions and may be affected by different or ageing instruments and different operators.
Distinguish accuracy from precision.
Understand precision as closeness of repeated measurements to one another, independently of closeness to the true value.
Recognise precision, Gauge R&R, or measurement error as a primary metric of measurement-system analysis.
Understand stability as measurement-system drift over time that can affect accuracy or precision.
Explain that stability can be monitored using repeated Gauge R&R studies and by plotting relevant measurement metrics over time.
Recognise a non-stable measurement system as inconsistent.
Understand linearity as variation in accuracy or precision across the operating domain of a measurement device.
Assess whether measurement error changes with the magnitude of the measured quantity.
Understand precision-to-total variation as a metric quantifying measurement error relative to total observed variation or tolerance, as defined in the study context.
Understand Percent R&R as a metric quantifying the proportion of total observed variation attributable to measurement-system variation.
Interpret P/TV and %RR as measures of the contribution of measurement error to observed variation; recognise %RR as statistically preferable in the lecture material.
Understand discrimination as the number of distinct measurement-system variation intervals or categories that fit within total variation.

Interpret gauge-study ratings: unacceptable when P/TV >30%, %RR >9%, or discrimination <5; bad when P/TV is 10–30%, %RR is 2–9%, or discrimination is 5–10; good when P/TV is 0–10%, %RR is 0–2%, or discrimination >10.
Recognise the study design of 3 operators, 3 repeats each, and 10 parts, giving 90 total runs, as an industry-standard arrangement presented in the lecture.

Interpret the variance components and gauge-evaluation sections of a Gauge R&R table, including repeatability, reproducibility, part-to-part variation, total variation, study variation, tolerance contribution, and number of distinct categories.
Use P/TV (SV/Tol) to address whether the measurement system can be used to inspect parts relative to tolerance specifications.
Use %RR (%SV) to address whether the measurement system can detect a process shift relative to process variation.

Interpret Gauge R&R visual outputs, including components of variation, range chart by operator, X-bar chart by operator, measurements by part, measurements by operator, and part-by-operator interaction.
